result: The cat sleeps on my keyboard, paws pressed into home-row, tail draped across the spacebar like silk ribbon. Outside, moonlight paints the roof silver and the wind chimes clack their wooden teeth. I think about how every story begins in the cartography of skin: freckles like archipelagos, scars like river deltas. We navigate ourselves by touch long before by sight. Last week I listened to a man on the train describe dawn in Okinawa—he said the horizon cracked open like a persimmon, spilling pomegranate light over the deck of his destroyer. I’d never seen admiral purple bruise into daylight, but now I carry it. Language is like that: a contagion passed ear to throat to page. Somewhere a girl who doesn’t yet know she’s a woman is writing her name on classroom fog with hesitant fingers; the mist rewrites itself moments later, but the trace of her remains inside the molecules. I want to tell her that nothing vanishes, only re-collides. The coffee grows tepid beside me; stars unfurl their ancient catalogues against blind infinity. Each constellation is a tomb-robber who forgot the way home. The fridge’s electric hum is a monk reciting his one sutra. And the cat dreams of canaries— I can tell by the twitch of whiskers dancing reflections of things that fly. I would trade tomorrow’s sunrise for one lucid paw-strike at cobalt sky, the sound of wings that exist only behind her amber eyelids. Instead, I keep typing letters no one will read because velocity is its own prayer.
